title: AGENTS.md
text: chmsflow R package harmonizes Canadian Health Measures Survey (CHMS) variables across Cycles 1–6 (2007–2019). Variable names, codings, survey methodology drift cycles; package produces analysis-ready, consistent variables. depends broader recodeflow ecosystem (sibling: cchsflow). recoding behaviour data-driven — defined CSVs, code — consumed runtime recodeflow::rec_with_table().

title: AGENTS.md
text: variables.csv — one row per harmonized variable. Key columns: variable, databaseStart (cycles applies ), variableStart (find cycle). variable-details.csv — one row per recode rule. Specifies raw values map harmonized values (per cycle), including category labels tagged-NA missing codes. read recodeflow::rec_with_table(data, "variable_name", variable_details = variable_details). also bundled lazy-loaded datasets (data(variables), data(variable_details)) via data-raw/prep-dummy-data.R. variableStart format conventions (recodeflow standard, documented inst/metadata/): - [varname] — name across cycles - cycle1::var1, [var2] — mixed: cycle1 uses var1, cycles default var2 (bracketed value default) - database::varname — explicit per-cycle name varies - DerivedVar::[var1, var2] — variable computed others R function (named recEnd Func::function_name) Range notation recStart/recEnd: [7,9] (inclusive), [18.5,25) (half-open), else (catch-). CHMS-specific quirk: Cycle 1 (2007-2009) often uses different variable names Cycles 2-6 (e.g., amsdmva1 vs ammdmva1), mixed format exists.

title: AGENTS.md
text: variable-details.csv references Func::name, R function lives R/ exported via NAMESPACE. Files organized health domain: alcohol.R, blood-pressure.R, cholesterol--obesity.R, diabetes.R, diet.R, exercise.R, family-history.R, income.R, kidney.R, smoking.R, plus data.R dataset documentation. Medication code split across three files scale per-cycle ATC handling: medications-predicates.R — is_atc_class() helper eight scalar ATC predicates (is_ace_inhibitor(), is_beta_blocker(), is_calcium_channel_blocker(), is_diuretic(), is_diabetes_med(), is_nsaid(), is_any_antihtn_med(), is_other_antihtn_med()) medications-cycles1to2.R — is_atc_class_cycles1to2() helper eight cycles 1–2 wrapper functions; wrapper preserves 80-argument signature signature load-bearing DerivedVar:: metadata contract variable-details.csv medications-recode.R — four DB wrappers (recode_meds_cycles1to2(), recode_meds_cycles3to6(), aggregate_meds_by_person(), recode_after_meds()) plus shared internal helpers Conventions functions: - Vectorised — accept scalars vectors equal length - Return haven::tagged_na("") “applicable” / tagged_na("b") “missing”; StatsCan codes 6/7/8/9 map specific tagged NAs - Use logger::log_* diagnostics rather message()/warning() - Documented roxygen2 (markdown enabled — see Roxygen: list(markdown = TRUE) DESCRIPTION)

title: Analysis walkthrough: hypertension prevalence
text: Use recode_after_meds() instead rec_with_table() variables depend medication status. automatically excludes medication-specific rows variable_details pre-computed medication columns passed rather re-derived. Recode to other"

title: Analysis walkthrough: hypertension prevalence
text: analyse multiple cycles, repeat steps 2–3 cycle, add cycle identifier, bind together.
code: # Repeat for cycles 4-6 cycle4 <- recode_meds_cycles3to6(cycle4, cycle4_meds, c("any_htn_med", "diab_med")) cycle5 <- recode_meds_cycles3to6(cycle5, cycle5_meds, c("any_htn_med", "diab_med")) cycle6 <- recode_meds_cycles3to6(cycle6, cycle6_meds, c("any_htn_med", "diab_med"))  cycle4_htn <- recode_after_meds(cycle4, htn_variables) cycle5_htn <- recode_after_meds(cycle5, htn_variables) cycle6_htn <- recode_after_meds(cycle6, htn_variables)  # Add cycle identifiers and combine cycle3_htn$cycle <- 3 cycle4_htn$cycle <- 4 cycle5_htn$cycle <- 5 cycle6_htn$cycle <- 6  combined <- bind_rows(cycle3_htn, cycle4_htn, cycle5_htn, cycle6_htn)  # Hypertension prevalence by cycle combined |>   filter(!is.na(htn_status)) |>   group_by(cycle) |>   summarise(     n = n(),     n_htn = sum(htn_status == 1),     prevalence = mean(htn_status == 1)   )

title: Derived variables
text: two types derived variables CHMS surveys. supported chmsflow. Variable mapping – mapping two variables single variable. Computed variables – variables derived using mathematical equations clinical logic. chmsflow computes derived variables using functions referenced variable-details.csv. recEnd column uses prefix Func:: name R function, variableStart column uses prefix DerivedVar:: list input variables. example, GFR (gfr_ml_min) : recEnd: Func::calculate_gfr variableStart: DerivedVar::[lab_bcre, pgdcgt, clc_sex, clc_age] tells rec_with_table() call calculate_gfr() four input variables.

title: Get started
text: Use rec_with_table() recodeflow transform CHMS variables. cycle data object must named cyclex recoding work properly.
code: library(recodeflow)  # Recode a source variable (age) cycle4_ages <- rec_with_table(   cycle4, "clc_age",   variable_details = variable_details, log = TRUE ) Using the passed data variable name as database_name The variable clc_age was recoded into clc_age for the database cycle4 the following recodes were made: value_to       From rows_recoded 1     copy    [3, 80]           50 2    NA::a        996            0 3    NA::b [997, 999]            0 4     <NA>       else            0 head(cycle4_ages) clc_age 1      73 2      33 3      22 4      47 5      74 6      22

title: Get started
text: chmsflow handles three types variables, recoded differently. Source variables mapped directly raw CHMS columns. Variable names may differ across cycles, chmsflow harmonizes single name. variables convert continuous measurements categories using thresholds defined variable-details.csv. Recode age into 4 groups cycle4_categorical_ages <- rec_with_table(   cycle4, "agegroup4",   variable_details = variable_details, log = TRUE ) Using the passed data variable name as database_name The variable clc_age was recoded into agegroup4 for the database cycle4 the following recodes were made: value_to       From rows_recoded 1        1   [20, 39]           12 2        2   [40, 59]           23 3        3   [60, 69]            6 4        4   [70, 79]            9 5    NA::a        996            0 6    NA::b [997, 999]            0 7    NA(b)       else            0 head(cycle4_categorical_ages) agegroup4 1         4 2         1 3         1 4         2 5         4 6         1 # code: CustomFunctionName <- function(Vars from variableStart following same order){   outputVar <- {Code on passed vars that generates a single value output}    return(outputVar) } calculate_pack_years <- function(smkdsty, clc_age, smk_54, smk_52, smk_31, smk_41, smk_53, smk_42, smk_21, smk_11) {   pack_years <- dplyr::case_when(     # Age: valid skip     clc_age == 96 ~ haven::tagged_na("a"),     # Age: don't know, refusal, not stated     clc_age < 0 | clc_age %in% 97:99 ~ haven::tagged_na("b"),      # Pack-years by smoking status     smkdsty == 1 ~ pmax(((clc_age - smk_52) * (smk_31 / 20)), 0.0137),     smkdsty == 2 ~ pmax(((clc_age - smk_52 - (clc_age - smk_54)) * (smk_53 / 20)), 0.0137) +       ((pmax((smk_41 * smk_42 / 30), 1) / 20) * (clc_age - smk_54)),     smkdsty == 3 ~ (pmax((smk_41 * smk_42 / 30), 1) / 20) * (clc_age - smk_21),     smkdsty == 4 ~ pmax(((smk_54 - smk_52) * (smk_53 / 20)), 0.0137),     smkdsty == 5 & smk_11 == 1 ~ 0.0137,     smkdsty == 5 & smk_11 == 2 ~ 0.007,     smkdsty == 6 ~ 0,      # Smoking status: valid skip     smkdsty == 96 ~ haven::tagged_na("a"),     # Smoking status: don't know, refusal, not stated     smkdsty %in% 97:99 ~ haven::tagged_na("b"),     .default = haven::tagged_na("b")   )   return(pack_years) }

title: Methodology
text: Canadian Health Measures Survey (CHMS) repeated cross-sectional survey conducted Statistics Canada since 2007. cycle collects questionnaire, physical measurement, laboratory data nationally representative sample Canadians. Combining data across cycles enables trend analyses increases statistical power, raw data directly comparable across cycles several reasons. Variable names sometimes change cycles. example, accelerometer variable moderate--vigorous physical activity day 1 named amsdmva1 cycle 1, ammdmva1 cycles 2–6. researcher pooling cycles must know every rename, risk silent misalignment. chmsflow handles renames metadata variable-details.csv. variableStart column uses mixed format specify cycle-specific exceptions: means: use amsdmva1 cycle 1, ammdmva1 cycles. recodeflow package reads format applies correct mapping automatically. full list variable naming patterns, see inst/metadata/schemas/chms/chms_database_config.yaml package source. Even variable names stable, coding categories may differ. CHMS response codes missing data, valid skips, refusals vary across variables cycles. chmsflow’s recoding rules variable-details.csv define explicit mappings response code, ensuring consistent treatment across cycles. Cycles 1–2 store medication data wide format: 80 columns ATC code time-last-taken pairs per respondent. Cycles 3–6 use long format: one row per medication per respondent two columns (meucatc npi_25b). chmsflow provides separate functions format (recode_meds_cycles1to2() recode_meds_cycles3to6()) identical call signatures, downstream workflow regardless cycle. See Recoding medications details.

title: Missing data (tagged_na)
text: Base R supports one type NA (‘available’) represent missing values. CHMS, however, several types missing data incomplete category responses. chmsflow uses haven package tagged_na() allow multiple missing data categories. tagged_na() adds addition character NA value, thereby allowing users define additional missing data types. tagged_na() applies numeric values, character base values can use string represent NA missing data.

title: Variable schema reference
text: chmsflow uses two CSV metadata files define raw CHMS variables harmonized. files bundled package inst/extdata/ also available data objects (variables variable_details). variables.csv – lists every harmonized variable name, label, type, unit variable-details.csv – defines row--row recoding rules rec_with_table() applies vignette column--column reference files. explanation files fit harmonization workflow, see Methodology.

title: Variable schema reference
text: 1. variable – name harmonized variable. 2. label – short label variable. 3. labelLong – detailed label variable. 4. section – broad grouping variable belongs (e.g., sociodemographics, health behaviour, health status). 5. subject – specific topic variable pertains (e.g., age, smoking, blood pressure). 6. variableType – whether harmonized variable Categorical Continuous. 7. units – units harmonized variable, N/unitless. 8. databaseStart – CHMS cycles contain variable, separated commas. 9. variableStart – source variable names listed CHMS cycle. Uses format conventions variable-details.csv (see ).

Uses format conventions variable-details.csv (see ).

title: Variable schema reference
text: row defines recoding rule one category one variable. categorical variable 4 categories, plus -applicable category, missing category, else row, 7 rows. Missing data rows use haven::tagged_na(): NA::– valid skip (applicable) NA::b – missing (don’t know, refusal, stated) else row catches values matched row. use clc_sex running example. 1. variable – name harmonized variable. 2. dummyVariable – dummy variable name category (categorical variables ; N/continuous). 3. typeEnd – variable type harmonized variable (cat cont). 4. databaseStart – CHMS cycles containing variable, separated commas. 5. variableStart – source variable names CHMS cycle. Supports several formats: 6. typeStart – variable type source CHMS data (cat cont). 7. recEnd – value recode category . Special values: copy – pass unchanged (continuous variables) NA::– applicable NA::b – missing Func::function_name – derived variable computed named function 8. numValidCat – number non-missing categories (categorical ; N/continuous). used rec_with_table(). 9. catLabel – short label category. 10. catLabelLong – detailed label, matching CHMS documentation possible. 11. units – units variable, N/. Must consistent across rows variable. 12. recStart – source value range match. Uses interval notation: [1, 4] – integer values 1 4 [1, 2.5] – values 1 2.5 (2.55 match) else – values matched rows copy – combined else, copies unmatched values unchanged 13. catStartLabel – label source category, matching CHMS documentation. missing rows, describes missing code value. 14. variableStartShortLabel – short label source variable. 15. variableStartLabel – detailed label source variable, matching CHMS documentation. 16. notes – relevant notes changes CHMS cycles, missing categories, variable type changes. Derived variables use two special column values: variableStart: DerivedVar::[var1, var2, var3] – lists input variables recEnd: Func::function_name – names R function computes derived variable See Derived variables details derived variables work.

code: **Missing Data Codes:**      - `996`: Valid skip (e.g., measurement not taken). Handled as `haven::tagged_na("a")`.      - `997-999`: Don't know, refusal, or not stated. Handled as `haven::tagged_na("b")`.

title: Adjusted diastolic blood pressure — adjust_dbp
code: # Scalar usage: Single respondent # Example: Adjust for a respondent with average diastolic blood pressure of 80 mmHg. adjust_dbp(bpmdpbpd = 80) #> [1] 82 # Output: 82  # Example: Adjust for a respondent with a non-response diastolic blood pressure of 996. result <- adjust_dbp(bpmdpbpd = 996) result # Shows: NA #> [1] NA haven::is_tagged_na(result, "a") # Shows: TRUE (confirms it's tagged NA(a)) #> [1] TRUE format(result, tag = TRUE) # Shows: "NA(a)" (displays the tag) #> [1] "NA"  # Multiple respondents adjust_dbp(bpmdpbpd = c(80, 90, 100)) #> [1] 82.0 90.3 98.6 # Returns: c(82, 90.3, 98.6)  # Database usage: Applied to survey datasets # library(dplyr) # dataset |> #   mutate(dbp_adj_mmhg = adjust_dbp(bpmdpbpd))

text: function processes physical activity data accelerometer measurements create weekly activity summary.
code: **Data Quality Requirements:**      - Requires complete 7-day data (missing days result in tagged NA)      - This conservative approach ensures reliable activity estimates      - Zero values are preserved (represent valid no-activity days)       **Missing Data Codes:**      - For all input variables:        - `9996`: Valid skip. Handled as `haven::tagged_na("a")`.        - `9997-9999`: Don't know, refusal, or not stated. Handled as `haven::tagged_na("b")`.

code: # Scalar usage: Single respondent # Example: Calculate the average minutes of exercise per day for a week of accelerometer data. calculate_exercise_daily_avg(30, 40, 25, 35, 20, 45, 50) #> [1] 35 # Output: 35  # Example: Respondent has non-response values for all inputs. result <- calculate_exercise_daily_avg(9998, 9998, 9998, 9998, 9998, 9998, 9998) result # Shows: NA #> [1] NA haven::is_tagged_na(result, "b") # Shows: TRUE (confirms it's tagged NA(b)) #> [1] TRUE format(result, tag = TRUE) # Shows: "NA(b)" (displays the tag) #> [1] "NA"  # Multiple respondents calculate_exercise_daily_avg(   c(30, 20), c(40, 30), c(25, 35), c(35, 45),   c(20, 25), c(45, 55), c(50, 60) ) #> [1] 35.00000 38.57143 # Returns: c(35, 39.28571)  # Database usage: Applied to survey datasets # library(dplyr) # dataset |> #   mutate(avg_exercise = calculate_exercise_daily_avg(ammdmva1, ammdmva2, #     ammdmva3, ammdmva4, ammdmva5, ammdmva6, ammdmva7))

title: Estimated glomerular filtration rate (GFR) — calculate_gfr
text: function calculates estimated glomerular filtration rate (GFR) according Finlay's formula, serum creatine mg/dL. calculation takes account respondent's ethnicity, sex, age.

title: Estimated glomerular filtration rate (GFR) — calculate_gfr
code: calculate_gfr(lab_bcre, pgdcgt, clc_sex, clc_age)

title: Estimated glomerular filtration rate (GFR) — calculate_gfr
text: lab_bcre numeric Blood creatine (umol/L). numeric 14 785. pgdcgt integer Ethnicity (13 categories). integer 1 13. clc_sex integer Sex (Male = 1, Female = 2). integer either 1 2. clc_age numeric Age (years). numeric 3 79.

title: Estimated glomerular filtration rate (GFR) — calculate_gfr
text: numeric calculated GFR. inputs invalid bounds, function returns tagged NA.

title: Estimated glomerular filtration rate (GFR) — calculate_gfr
text: function implements Modification Diet Renal Disease (MDRD) equation estimate glomerular filtration rate, key indicator kidney function.
code: **Clinical Significance:**      GFR estimates are essential for:      - Chronic kidney disease (CKD) classification      - Medication dosing adjustments      - Cardiovascular risk assessment       **Formula Application:**      Base: GFR = 175 x (creatinine^-1.154) x (age^-0.203)      Adjustments:      - Female: x 0.742      - Black ethnicity: x 1.210       **Unit Conversion:**      Serum creatinine converted from umol/L to mg/dL (/ 88.4)       **Missing Data Codes:**      - `lab_bcre`: `9996` (Not applicable), `9997-9999` (Missing)      - `pgdcgt`: `96` (Not applicable), `97-99` (Missing)      - `clc_sex`: `6` (Not applicable), `7-9` (Missing)      - `clc_age`: `96` (Not applicable), `97-99` (Missing)

title: Estimated glomerular filtration rate (GFR) — calculate_gfr
code: # Scalar usage: Single respondent # Example 1: Calculate gfr for a 45-year-old white female with serum creatine of 80 umol/L. calculate_gfr(lab_bcre = 80, pgdcgt = 1, clc_sex = 2, clc_age = 45) #> [1] 67.27905 # Output: 67.27905  # Example 2: Respondent has non-response values for all inputs. result <- calculate_gfr(lab_bcre = 9998, pgdcgt = 98, clc_sex = 8, clc_age = 98) result # Shows: NA #> [1] NA haven::is_tagged_na(result, "b") # Shows: TRUE (confirms it's tagged NA(b)) #> [1] TRUE format(result, tag = TRUE) # Shows: "NA(b)" (displays the tag) #> [1] "NA"  # Multiple respondents calculate_gfr(   lab_bcre = c(80, 70, 90), pgdcgt = c(1, 2, 1),   clc_sex = c(2, 2, 1), clc_age = c(45, 35, 50) ) #> [1] 67.27905 99.94114 77.47422 # Returns: c(67.27905, 99.94114, 70.38001)  # Database usage: Applied to survey datasets # library(dplyr) # dataset |> #   mutate(gfr = calculate_gfr(lab_bcre, pgdcgt, clc_sex, clc_age))

title: Adjusted total household income — calculate_household_income

Handled as `haven::tagged_na("a")`.        - `99999997-99999999`: Don't know, refusal, or not stated. Handled as `haven::tagged_na("b")`.      - `dhhdhsz`:        - `96`: Valid skip. Handled as `haven::tagged_na("a")`.        - `97-99`: Don't know, refusal, or not stated. Handled as `haven::tagged_na("b")`.

title: Adjusted total household income — calculate_household_income
code: # Scalar usage: Single respondent # Example 1: Respondent with $50,000 income and a household size of 3. calculate_household_income(thi_01 = 50000, dhhdhsz = 3) #> [1] 29411.76 # Output: 29411.76  # Example 2: Respondent has non-response values for all inputs. result <- calculate_household_income(thi_01 = 99999998, dhhdhsz = 98) result # Shows: NA #> [1] NA haven::is_tagged_na(result, "b") # Shows: TRUE (confirms it's tagged NA(b)) #> [1] TRUE format(result, tag = TRUE) # Shows: "NA(b)" (displays the tag) #> [1] "NA"  # Multiple respondents calculate_household_income(thi_01 = c(50000, 75000, 90000), dhhdhsz = c(3, 2, 1)) #> [1] 29411.76 53571.43 90000.00 # Returns: c(29411.76, 53571.43, 90000)  # Database usage: Applied to survey datasets # library(dplyr) # dataset |> #   mutate(adj_hh_income = calculate_household_income(thi_01, dhhdhsz))

title: Smoking pack-years — calculate_pack_years
text: smkdsty integer integer representing smoking status respondent. clc_age numeric numeric representing respondent's age. smk_54 numeric numeric representing respondent's age stopped smoking daily. smk_52 numeric numeric representing respondent's age first started smoking daily. smk_31 integer integer representing number cigarettes smoked per day daily smokers. smk_41 numeric numeric representing number cigarettes smoked per day occasional smokers. smk_53 numeric numeric representing number cigarettes smoked per day former daily smokers. smk_42 numeric numeric representing number days past month respondent smoked least 1 cigarette (occasional smokers). smk_21 numeric numeric representing respondent's age first started smoking occasionally. smk_11 integer integer representing whether respondent smoked least 100 cigarettes lifetime.

title: Smoking pack-years — calculate_pack_years
text: numeric numeric representing pack years respondent's smoking history. inputs invalid bounds, function returns tagged NA.

title: Smoking pack-years — calculate_pack_years
text: Pack-years standardized measure lifetime cigarette exposure used epidemiological research clinical practice. calculation varies smoking pattern:
code: **Smoking Patterns:**      - Daily smokers: Consistent daily consumption over time period      - Occasional smokers: Variable consumption adjusted for frequency      - Former smokers: Historical consumption during smoking periods       **Minimum Values:**      The function applies minimum pack-year values (0.0137 or 0.007) to prevent      underestimation of health risks for light smokers.       **Missing Data Codes:**      - `smkdsty`: `96` (Not applicable), `97-99` (Missing)      - `clc_age`: `96` (Not applicable), `97-99` (Missing)      - Other variables: Handled within the formula logic.

title: Smoking pack-years — calculate_pack_years
code: # Scalar usage: Single respondent # A former occasional smoker who smoked at least 100 cigarettes in their lifetime. calculate_pack_years(   smkdsty = 5, clc_age = 50, smk_54 = 40, smk_52 = 18, smk_31 = NA,   smk_41 = 15, smk_53 = NA, smk_42 = 3, smk_21 = 25, smk_11 = 1 ) #> [1] 0.0137 # Output: 0.0137  # Example: Respondent has non-response values for all inputs. result <- calculate_pack_years(   smkdsty = 98, clc_age = 998, smk_54 = 98, smk_52 = 98, smk_31 = 98,   smk_41 = 98, smk_53 = 98, smk_42 = 98, smk_21 = 98, smk_11 = 8 ) result # Shows: NA #> [1] NA haven::is_tagged_na(result, "b") # Shows: TRUE (confirms it's tagged NA(b)) #> [1] TRUE format(result, tag = TRUE) # Shows: "NA(b)" (displays the tag) #> [1] "NA"  # Multiple respondents calculate_pack_years(   smkdsty = c(1, 5, 6),   clc_age = c(40, 50, 60),   smk_52 = c(20, 18, NA),   smk_31 = c(30, NA, NA),   smk_54 = c(NA, 40, NA),   smk_41 = c(NA, 15, NA),   smk_53 = c(NA, NA, NA),   smk_42 = c(NA, 3, NA),   smk_21 = c(NA, 25, NA),   smk_11 = c(NA, 1, NA) ) #> [1] 30.0000  0.0137  0.0000 # Returns: c(30, 0.0137, 0)  # Database usage: Applied to survey datasets # library(dplyr) # dataset |> #   mutate(pack_years = calculate_pack_years(smkdsty, clc_age, smk_54, smk_52, #     smk_31, smk_41, smk_53, smk_42, smk_21, smk_11))

title: Low risk drinking score — derive_alcohol_risk
text: function calculates low drink score (step 1 ) respondent using Canada's Low-Risk Alcohol Drinking Guideline. score based solely number standard drinks consumed per week respondent's sex. (Step 2, add additional points based drinking habits, included.).

title: Low risk drinking score — derive_alcohol_risk
code: derive_alcohol_risk(clc_sex, alc_11, alcdwky)

title: Low risk drinking score — derive_alcohol_risk
text: clc_sex integer integer indicating respondent's sex (1 male, 2 female). alc_11 integer integer indicating whether respondent drank alcohol past year (1 "Yes", 2 ""). alcdwky integer integer representing number standard drinks consumed respondent week.

title: Low risk drinking score — derive_alcohol_risk
text: integer low drink score, : 1 "Low risk" (0 points), 2 "Marginal risk" (1-2 points), 3 "Medium risk" (3-4 points), 4 "High risk" (5-9 points). inputs invalid bounds, function returns tagged NA.

title: Low risk drinking score — derive_alcohol_risk
text: scoring determined first allocating points (referred step1) based weekly alcohol consumption respondent's sex: respondent drank past year (alc_11 == 1): alcdwky <= 10, assign 0 points. alcdwky > 10 <= 15: assign 0 points males (clc_sex == 1) 1 point females (clc_sex == 2). alcdwky > 15 <= 20: assign 1 point males 3 points females. alcdwky > 20: assign 3 points. respondents drink past year (alc_11 == 2), 0 points assigned. step1 points mapped final categorical score follows: 0 points -> score 1 ("Low risk"), 1-2 points -> score 2 ("Marginal risk"), 3-4 points -> score 3 ("Medium risk"), 5-9 points -> score 4 ("High risk"). function implements Canada's Low-Risk Alcohol Drinking Guidelines (Step 1 ) assess alcohol consumption risk. scoring system considers quantity alcohol consumed biological sex differences alcohol metabolism. Risk Categories: Low risk (0 points): Safe consumption levels Marginal risk (1-2 points): Slightly elevated risk Medium risk (3-4 points): Moderate health concerns High risk (5-9 points): Significant health risks Sex-Based Differences: Women generally lower tolerance thresholds due physiological differences alcohol metabolism, reflected sex-specific point allocations. Non-response Handling: Invalid inputs survey non-response values result tagged NA ("b").

title: Low risk drinking score — derive_alcohol_risk
text: function implements Step 1 guidelines. Step 2 (additional drinking pattern assessments) included due data limitations survey.

title: Low risk drinking score — derive_alcohol_risk
text: Canada's Low-Risk Alcohol Drinking Guidelines, Health Canada

title: Low risk drinking score — derive_alcohol_risk
code: # Scalar usage: Single respondent # Example: A male respondent who drank in the past year and consumes 3 standard drinks per week. derive_alcohol_risk(clc_sex = 1, alc_11 = 1, alcdwky = 3) #> [1] 1 # Expected output: 1 (Low risk)  # Missing data examples showing tagged NA patterns result <- derive_alcohol_risk(clc_sex = 1, alc_11 = 6, alcdwky = 5) result # Shows: NA #> [1] NA haven::is_tagged_na(result, "a") # Shows: TRUE (confirms it's tagged NA(a)) #> [1] TRUE format(result, tag = TRUE) # Shows: "NA(a)" (displays the tag) #> [1] "NA"  result <- derive_alcohol_risk(clc_sex = 1, alc_11 = 7, alcdwky = 5) result # Shows: NA #> [1] NA haven::is_tagged_na(result, "b") # Shows: TRUE (confirms it's tagged NA(b)) #> [1] TRUE format(result, tag = TRUE) # Shows: "NA(b)" (displays the tag) #> [1] "NA"  result <- derive_alcohol_risk(clc_sex = 1, alc_11 = 1, alcdwky = NA) result # Shows: NA #> [1] NA haven::is_tagged_na(result, "b") # Shows: TRUE (confirms it's tagged NA(b)) #> [1] TRUE format(result, tag = TRUE) # Shows: "NA(b)" (displays the tag) #> [1] "NA"  # Multiple respondents derive_alcohol_risk(clc_sex = c(1, 2, 1), alc_11 = c(1, 1, 2), alcdwky = c(3, 12, NA)) #> [1] 1 2 1 # Returns: c(1, 2, 1)  # Database usage: Applied to survey datasets # library(dplyr) # dataset |> #   mutate(alc_risk_score = derive_alcohol_risk(clc_sex, alc_11, alcdwky))

title: Low risk drinking score - former/never categories — derive_alcohol_risk_detailed
text: Computes categorical alcohol consumption score based Canada's Low-Risk Alcohol Drinking Guidelines (Step 1), distinguishing never, former, light, moderate, heavy drinkers. function uses information weekly consumption, past-year use, lifetime drinking, history heavy drinking.

title: Low risk drinking score - former/never categories — derive_alcohol_risk_detailed
code: derive_alcohol_risk_detailed(clc_sex, alc_11, alcdwky, alc_17, alc_18)

title: Low risk drinking score - former/never categories — derive_alcohol_risk_detailed
text: clc_sex integer Respondent's sex (1 = male, 2 = female). alc_11 integer Whether respondent drank alcohol past year (1 = Yes, 2 = ). alcdwky integer Number standard drinks consumed typical week (0-84). alc_17 integer Whether respondent ever drank alcohol lifetime (1 = Yes, 2 = ). alc_18 integer Whether respondent regularly drank 12 drinks per week (1 = Yes, 2 = ).

title: Low risk drinking score - former/never categories — derive_alcohol_risk_detailed
text: integer Score: 1 = Never drank, 2 = Low-risk (former light) drinker, 3 = Moderate drinker (1–2 points), 4 = Heavy drinker (3–4 points). inputs invalid bounds, function returns tagged NA.

title: Low risk drinking score - former/never categories — derive_alcohol_risk_detailed
text: Step 1: Assign points based weekly alcohol consumption. respondent drank past year (alc_11 == 1): 0 10 drinks/week: 0 points 11 15 drinks/week: 0 points males, 1 point females 16 20 drinks/week: 1 point males, 3 points females 20 drinks/week: 3 points males, 5 points females drink past year (alc_11 == 2): 0 points Step 2: Determine final categorical score. point score Step 1 0, final category determined based lifetime past-year drinking habits: score 1 (Never drinker) assigned respondent either never drank alcohol lifetime former drinker regularly consume 12 drinks week. score 2 (Low-risk drinker) assigned respondent drank past year (still scored 0 points) former drinker history regularly consuming 12 drinks week. point score Step 1 1 2, respondent classified Moderate drinker (Score = 3). point score Step 1 3 , respondent classified Heavy drinker (Score = 4). inputs invalid bounds, function returns tagged NA.

title: Low risk drinking score - former/never categories — derive_alcohol_risk_detailed
text: function uses Step 1 guidelines, Step 2 information unavailable CHMS.

title: Low risk drinking score - former/never categories — derive_alcohol_risk_detailed
text: Canada's Low-Risk Alcohol Drinking Guidelines, Health Canada

title: Low risk drinking score - former/never categories — derive_alcohol_risk_detailed
code: # Scalar usage: Single respondent # Example: Male, drinks 3 drinks/week, drank in past year, no history of heavy drinking derive_alcohol_risk_detailed(   clc_sex = 1, alc_11 = 1, alcdwky = 3,   alc_17 = 1, alc_18 = 2 ) #> [1] 2 # Expected output: 2  # Missing data examples showing tagged NA patterns result <- derive_alcohol_risk_detailed(   clc_sex = 1, alc_11 = 6, alcdwky = 5,   alc_17 = 1, alc_18 = 2 ) result # Shows: NA #> [1] NA haven::is_tagged_na(result, "a") # Shows: TRUE (confirms it's tagged NA(a)) #> [1] TRUE format(result, tag = TRUE) # Shows: "NA(a)" (displays the tag) #> [1] "NA"  result <- derive_alcohol_risk_detailed(   clc_sex = 1, alc_11 = 7, alcdwky = 5,   alc_17 = 1, alc_18 = 2 ) result # Shows: NA #> [1] NA haven::is_tagged_na(result, "b") # Shows: TRUE (confirms it's tagged NA(b)) #> [1] TRUE format(result, tag = TRUE) # Shows: "NA(b)" (displays the tag) #> [1] "NA"  result <- derive_alcohol_risk_detailed(   clc_sex = 1, alc_11 = 1, alcdwky = NA,   alc_17 = 1, alc_18 = 2 ) result # Shows: NA #> [1] NA haven::is_tagged_na(result, "b") # Shows: TRUE (confirms it's tagged NA(b)) #> [1] TRUE format(result, tag = TRUE) # Shows: "NA(b)" (displays the tag) #> [1] "NA"  # Multiple respondents derive_alcohol_risk_detailed(   clc_sex = c(1, 2, 1), alc_11 = c(1, 1, 2),   alcdwky = c(3, 12, NA), alc_17 = c(1, 1, 1), alc_18 = c(2, 2, 1) ) #> [1] 2 3 2 # Returns: c(2, 3, 2)  # Database usage: Applied to survey datasets # library(dplyr) # dataset |> #   mutate( #     alc_detailed_risk_score = derive_alcohol_risk_detailed( #       clc_sex, alc_11, alcdwky, alc_17, alc_18 #     ) #   )

title: Diabetes derived variable — derive_diabetes_status
text: function evaluates diabetes status using comprehensive approach combines laboratory measurements, self-reported diagnosis, medication usage create inclusive diabetes classification.

title: Diabetes derived variable — derive_diabetes_status
code: derive_diabetes_status(diab_a1c, ccc_51, diab_med)

title: Diabetes derived variable — derive_diabetes_status
text: diab_a1c integer integer indicating whether respondent diabetes based HbA1c level. 1 "Yes", 2 "". ccc_51 integer integer indicating whether respondent self-reported diabetes. 1 "Yes", 2 "". diab_med integer integer indicating whether respondent diabetes medication. 1 "Yes", 0 "".

title: Diabetes derived variable — derive_diabetes_status
text: integer inclusive diabetes status: - 1 ("Yes") diab_a1c, ccc_51, diab_med 1. - 2 ("") diab_a1c, ccc_51, diab_med 2 0. - haven::tagged_na(""): applicable - haven::tagged_na("b"): Missing

title: Diabetes derived variable — derive_diabetes_status
text: function classifies diabetes status based considers:
code: **Data Sources:**      - Laboratory: HbA1c levels indicating diabetes (diab_a1c)      - Self-report: Participant-reported diabetes diagnosis (ccc_51)      - Medication: Current diabetes medication usage (diab_med)       **Classification Logic:**      - ANY positive indicator results in diabetes classification      - ALL negative indicators required for "no diabetes" classification      - Sophisticated missing data handling preserves available information       **Missing Data Codes:**      - `diab_a1c`, `diab_med`:        - Tagged NA "a": Valid skip.        - Tagged NA "b": Don't know, refusal, or not stated.      - `ccc_51`:        - `6`: Valid skip. Handled as `haven::tagged_na("a")`.        - `7-9`: Don't know, refusal, or not stated. Handled as `haven::tagged_na("b")`.

title: Diabetes derived variable — derive_diabetes_status
code: # Scalar usage: Single respondent # Example: Determine the inclusive diabetes status for a respondent with diabetes based on HbA1c. derive_diabetes_status(diab_a1c = 1, ccc_51 = 2, diab_med = 0) #> [1] 1 # Output: 1 (Inclusive diabetes status is "Yes").  # Example: Determine the inclusive diabetes status for a respondent no diabetes all around. derive_diabetes_status(diab_a1c = 2, ccc_51 = 2, diab_med = 0) #> [1] 2 # Output: 2 (Inclusive diabetes status is "No").  # Example: Determine inclusive diabetes status when only one parameter is NA. derive_diabetes_status(diab_a1c = 2, ccc_51 = NA, diab_med = 1) #> [1] 1 # Output: 1 (Based on `diab_med`, inclusive diabetes status is "Yes").  # Example: Respondent has non-response values for all inputs. result <- derive_diabetes_status(haven::tagged_na("b"), 8, haven::tagged_na("b")) result # Shows: NA #> [1] NA haven::is_tagged_na(result, "b") # Shows: TRUE (confirms it's tagged NA(b)) #> [1] TRUE format(result, tag = TRUE) # Shows: "NA(b)" (displays the tag) #> [1] "NA"  # Multiple respondents derive_diabetes_status(diab_a1c = c(1, 2, 2), ccc_51 = c(2, 1, 2), diab_med = c(0, 0, 1)) #> [1] 1 1 1 # Returns: c(1, 1, 1)  # Database usage: Applied to survey datasets # library(dplyr) # dataset |> #   mutate(diabetes_status = derive_diabetes_status(diab_a1c, ccc_51, diab_med))

title: Hypertension derived variable — derive_hypertension
text: bpmdpbps integer integer representing systolic blood pressure measurement respondent. bpmdpbpd integer integer representing diastolic blood pressure measurement respondent. any_htn_med integer integer indicating whether respondent medication hypertension. 1: Yes 0: ccc_32 integer optional integer indicating whether respondent actually medication hypertension. 1: Yes 2: (default) cvd_status integer optional integer indicating presence cardiovascular disease, affecting medication status. 1: Yes 2: (default) diab_status integer optional integer indicating presence diabetes, affecting blood pressure thresholds. 1: Yes 2: (default) ckd_status integer optional integer indicating presence chronic kidney disease, affecting blood pressure thresholds. 1: Yes 2: (default)

title: Hypertension derived variable — derive_hypertension
text: integer hypertension status: 1: High blood pressure (BP >= 140/90 mmHg (>= 130/80 mmHg diabetes CKD) hypertension medication) 2: Normal blood pressure (BP < 140/90 mmHg (< 130/80 mmHg diabetes CKD) hypertension medication) haven::tagged_na(""): applicable haven::tagged_na("b"): Missing

title: Hypertension derived variable — derive_hypertension
text: function implements clinical guidelines hypertension classification:
code: **Blood Pressure Thresholds:**      - General population: >= 140/90 mmHg indicates hypertension      - Diabetes or CKD patients: >= 130/80 mmHg indicates hypertension (lower threshold)       **Medication Logic:**      - Anyone taking hypertension medication is classified as hypertensive      - Medication status may be adjusted based on comorbidities (diabetes, CKD, cardiovascular disease)       **Missing Data Codes:**      - `bpmdpbps`, `bpmdpbpd`:        - `996`: Valid skip. Handled as `haven::tagged_na("a")`.        - `997-999`: Don't know, refusal, or not stated. Handled as `haven::tagged_na("b")`.      - `any_htn_med`:        - Tagged NA "a": Valid skip.        - Tagged NA "b": Don't know, refusal, or not stated.      - `ccc_32`, `cvd_status`, `diab_status`, `ckd_status`:        - `6`: Valid skip. Handled as `haven::tagged_na("a")`.        - `7-9`: Don't know, refusal, or not stated. Handled as `haven::tagged_na("b")`.

title: Hypertension derived variable — derive_hypertension
code: # Scalar usage: Single respondent # Example 1: Respondent has systolic BP = 150, diastolic BP = 95, and on medication. derive_hypertension(bpmdpbps = 150, bpmdpbpd = 95, any_htn_med = 1) #> [1] 1 # Output: 1 (High blood pressure due to systolic BP, diastolic BP, and medication usage).  # Example 2: Respondent has systolic BP = 120, diastolic BP = 80, and not on medication. derive_hypertension(bpmdpbps = 120, bpmdpbpd = 80, any_htn_med = 0) #> [1] 2 # Output: 2 (Normal blood pressure as BP is below 140/90 mmHg and not on medication).  # Example 3: Respondent has non-response BP values of 996 for both systolic and diastolic. result <- derive_hypertension(bpmdpbps = 996, bpmdpbpd = 996, any_htn_med = 0) result # Shows: NA #> [1] NA haven::is_tagged_na(result, "a") # Shows: TRUE (confirms it's tagged NA(a)) #> [1] TRUE format(result, tag = TRUE) # Shows: "NA(a)" (displays the tag) #> [1] "NA"  # Multiple respondents derive_hypertension(   bpmdpbps = c(150, 120, 135), bpmdpbpd = c(95, 80, 85),   any_htn_med = c(1, 0, 1), diab_status = c(2, 2, 1) ) #> [1] 1 2 1 # Returns: c(1, 2, 1)  # Database usage: Applied to survey datasets # library(dplyr) # dataset |> #   mutate(hypertension = derive_hypertension(bpmdpbps, bpmdpbpd, any_htn_med))

title: Hypertension derived variable with adjusted blood pressures — derive_hypertension_adj
text: integer hypertension status: 1: High blood pressure (adjusted BP >= 140/90 mmHg (>= 130/80 mmHg diabetes CKD) hypertension medication) 2: Normal blood pressure (adjusted BP < 140/90 mmHg (< 130/80 mmHg diabetes CKD) hypertension medication) haven::tagged_na(""): applicable haven::tagged_na("b"): Missing

title: Hypertension derived variable with adjusted blood pressures — derive_hypertension_adj
text: function implements clinical guidelines hypertension classification using adjusted blood pressure values:
code: **Blood Pressure Thresholds:**      - General population: >= 140/90 mmHg indicates hypertension      - Diabetes or CKD patients: >= 130/80 mmHg indicates hypertension (lower threshold)       **Medication Logic:**      - Anyone taking hypertension medication is classified as hypertensive      - Medication status may be adjusted based on comorbidities (diabetes, CKD, cardiovascular disease)       **Missing Data Codes:**      - `sbp_adj_mmhg`, `dbp_adj_mmhg`:        - `996`: Valid skip. Handled as `haven::tagged_na("a")`.        - `997-999`: Don't know, refusal, or not stated. Handled as `haven::tagged_na("b")`.      - `any_htn_med`:        - Tagged NA "a": Valid skip.        - Tagged NA "b": Don't know, refusal, or not stated.      - `ccc_32`, `cvd_status`, `diab_status`, `ckd_status`:        - `6`: Valid skip. Handled as `haven::tagged_na("a")`.        - `7-9`: Don't know, refusal, or not stated. Handled as `haven::tagged_na("b")`.

title: Hypertension derived variable with adjusted blood pressures — derive_hypertension_adj
code: # Scalar usage: Single respondent # Example 1: Respondent has adjusted SBP = 150, adjusted DBP = 95, and on medication. derive_hypertension_adj(sbp_adj_mmhg = 150, dbp_adj_mmhg = 95, any_htn_med = 1) #> [1] 1 # Output: 1 (High blood pressure due to adjusted SBP, adjusted DBP, and medication usage).  # Example 2: Respondent has adjusted SBP = 120, adjusted DBP = 80, and not on medication. derive_hypertension_adj(sbp_adj_mmhg = 120, dbp_adj_mmhg = 80, any_htn_med = 2) #> [1] NA # Output: 2 (Normal blood pressure as adjusted BP is below 140/90 mmHg and not on medication).  # Example 3: Respondent has non-response BP values of 996 for both systolic and diastolic. result <- derive_hypertension_adj(sbp_adj_mmhg = 996, dbp_adj_mmhg = 996, any_htn_med = 0) result # Shows: NA #> [1] NA haven::is_tagged_na(result, "a") # Shows: TRUE (confirms it's tagged NA(a)) #> [1] TRUE format(result, tag = TRUE) # Shows: "NA(a)" (displays the tag) #> [1] "NA"  # Multiple respondents derive_hypertension_adj(   sbp_adj_mmhg = c(150, 120, 135), dbp_adj_mmhg = c(95, 80, 85),   any_htn_med = c(1, 0, 1), diab_status = c(2, 2, 1) ) #> [1] 1 2 1 # Returns: c(1, 2, 1)  # Database usage: Applied to survey datasets # library(dplyr) # dataset |> #   mutate(hypertension_adj = derive_hypertension_adj(sbp_adj_mmhg, dbp_adj_mmhg, any_htn_med))

title: Controlled hypertension derived variable — derive_hypertension_control
text: function assesses whether respondent's hypertension controlled:
code: **Control Thresholds:**      - General population: < 140/90 mmHg      - Diabetes or CKD patients: < 130/80 mmHg       **Logic:**      - Only applies to respondents taking hypertension medication.      - If BP is below the threshold, hypertension is "controlled" (1).      - If BP is at or above the threshold, it is "not controlled" (2).       **Missing Data Codes:**      - `bpmdpbps`, `bpmdpbpd`:        - `996`: Valid skip. Handled as `haven::tagged_na("a")`.        - `997-999`: Don't know, refusal, or not stated. Handled as `haven::tagged_na("b")`.      - `any_htn_med`:        - Tagged NA "a": Valid skip.        - Tagged NA "b": Don't know, refusal, or not stated.      - `ccc_32`, `cvd_status`, `diab_status`, `ckd_status`:        - `6`: Valid skip. Handled as `haven::tagged_na("a")`.        - `7-9`: Don't know, refusal, or not stated. Handled as `haven::tagged_na("b")`.

title: Controlled hypertension derived variable — derive_hypertension_control
code: # Scalar usage: Single respondent # Example 1: Respondent has systolic BP = 150, diastolic BP = 95, and on medication. derive_hypertension_control(bpmdpbps = 150, bpmdpbpd = 95, any_htn_med = 1) #> [1] 2 # Output: 2 (Hypertension not controlled due to high SBP and SBP despite medication usage).  # Example 2: Respondent has systolic BP = 120, diastolic BP = 80, and on medication. derive_hypertension_control(bpmdpbps = 120, bpmdpbpd = 80, any_htn_med = 1) #> [1] 1 # Output: 1 (Hypertension controlled as BP is below 140/90 mmHg and on medication).  # Example 3: Respondent has non-response BP values of 996 for both systolic and diastolic. result <- derive_hypertension_control(bpmdpbps = 996, bpmdpbpd = 996, any_htn_med = 0) result # Shows: NA #> [1] 2 haven::is_tagged_na(result, "a") # Shows: TRUE (confirms it's tagged NA(a)) #> [1] FALSE format(result, tag = TRUE) # Shows: "NA(a)" (displays the tag) #> [1] "2"  # Multiple respondents derive_hypertension_control(   bpmdpbps = c(150, 120, 135), bpmdpbpd = c(95, 80, 85),   any_htn_med = c(1, 1, 1), diab_status = c(2, 2, 1) ) #> [1] 2 1 2 # Returns: c(2, 1, 2)  # Database usage: Applied to survey datasets # library(dplyr) # dataset |> #   mutate(controlled_htn = derive_hypertension_control(bpmdpbps, bpmdpbpd, any_htn_med))

title: Beta blockers — is_beta_blocker
text: Identifies beta blockers based ATC codes starting "C07", excluding specific sub-codes.

title: Beta blockers — is_beta_blocker
code: # Scalar usage: Single respondent is_beta_blocker("C07AA13", 3) #> [1] 1 # Returns: 1  # Example: Respondent has non-response values for all inputs. result <- is_beta_blocker("9999998", 8) result # Shows: NA #> [1] NA haven::is_tagged_na(result, "b") # Shows: TRUE (confirms it's tagged NA(b)) #> [1] TRUE format(result, tag = TRUE) # Shows: "NA(b)" (displays the tag) #> [1] "NA"  # Multiple respondents is_beta_blocker(c("C07AA13", "C07AA07"), c(3, 4)) #> [1] 1 0 # Returns: c(1, 0)  # Database usage: Applied to survey datasets # library(dplyr) # dataset |> #   mutate(beta_blocker = is_beta_blocker(meucatc, npi_25b))

title: chmsflow 0.1.0
text: Added variables.csv contains list CHMS variables chmsflow. Added variable-details.csv maps variables across CHMS cycles 1-6 Added Vignettes describe process using rec_with_table() (part recodeflow) combine data

title: chmsflow 0.1.0
text: recode_meds_cycles1to2() recode_meds_cycles3to6() recode medication data merge medication columns main cycle data frame one call. share call signature, workflow identical regardless cycle: aggregate_meds_by_person() collapses long-format medication data one row per respondent, preserving tagged_na("") (valid skip) tagged_na("b") (missing) distinctions across aggregation. recode_after_meds() recodes derived variables (e.g. htn_status, diab_status) whose inputs include pre-computed medication variables, automatically excluding meds-database rows variable_details medication columns passed rather re-derived raw ATC/MHR columns. 16 ATC-classification functions (is_ace_inhibitor(), is_beta_blocker(), is_calcium_channel_blocker(), is_diuretic(), is_diabetes_med(), is_nsaid(), is_any_antihtn_med(), is_other_antihtn_med(), plus is_*_cycles1to2() variants) exported custom workflows. New vignette recoding_medications.qmd documents medication-recoding workflow cycle groupings, section calling underlying is_* classifiers directly.
